CLARITY.justification: Overall the paper is well organized (Intro → formulation → theory → algorithm → experiments → conclusion) and the three main contributions are stated early in the Introduction. Notation is mostly consistent and definitions/theorems are clearly presented (e.g. Definitions 1–4 and Theorems 1–6), which aids readability. Presentation issues are minor but noticeable: the provided text contains duplicated/garbled passages (especially in the Appendix/D section), occasional formatting glitches (missing/duplicated equations and stray TeX), and figures/tables are referenced but not present in the excerpt, which harms the reader’s ability to follow experimental claims. With those fixes the paper would be very clear.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a clearly new perspective—formalizing the "inference gap" between dropout’s stochastic ensemble and the common deterministic scaled-weight approximation, and introducing the notion of expectation-linearity (§4) to quantify it. It also proposes a concrete, simple regularizer (expectation-linearization, §5) to control that gap and provides a suite of theoretical bounds (Theorems 2–6 and a uniform deviation bound in §5.1) connecting gap size to layer/operator norms and max-norm practice. While related work (e.g. MC dropout, Gal & Ghahramani, and dropout distillation by Bulò et al.) addresses ensemble approximation or Bayesian views, the explicit definition, analysis, and regularization of the inference gap appears novel and clearly differentiated in the paper (cf. §2.3, §3, §5). Overall this is a notable, non-trivial conceptual and algorithmic contribution rather than a minor tweak.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Overall the paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported: The LVM view and Jensen-based Theorem 1 are correct (App. A), the layer-wise and deep-network expectation-linearity bounds (Theorems 2 and 3, App. B) follow from plausible operator-norm/Taylor arguments, and the uniform deviation (Theorem 4, App. C.1) uses standard Rademacher techniques and prior complexity bounds. Experimental methodology compares to sensible baselines (MC dropout, dropout distillation), reports mean±std over multiple runs, and explores λ. Minor issues remain: several results rely on strong or somewhat restrictive assumptions (e.g. unique best prediction and p(y|x;θ̂)>0 in Theorem 5, the Bγ<1 regime and bounded-op norm assumptions for deep ReLU nets), constants/bounds are fairly loose and some proofs use crude matrix bounds (Gershgorin), and experiments use only 5 repeats with no formal significance tests. These do not invalidate the main conclusions but warrant caveats.
